content.text: JavaScript was originally confined to the browser, where it animated pages and responded to user clicks. Node.js changed this in 2009 by letting JavaScript run directly on a server: reading files, handling HTTP connections, querying databases, and building complete backend services — all in the same language used in the browser.
Server-side JavaScript is JavaScript code that executes inside a server process rather than inside a web browser. The runtime that makes this possible is
— an open-source environment built by Ryan Dahl and first released in 2009. Node.js embeds Google's

JavaScript engine (the same engine that powers Chrome) into a standalone binary, adding APIs for the operating system that browsers intentionally omit: file system access, raw TCP/UDP sockets, child process spawning, and more.

Before Node.js, running server-side code required a completely separate language. Developers building a web product typically wrote their frontend in JavaScript and their backend in PHP, Python, Ruby, or Java. Node.js removed that split: JavaScript now works on both sides of the network boundary.

Node.js is not a web framework. It is a runtime — the underlying environment in which JavaScript programs execute. Frameworks like Express, Fastify, and NestJS are built on top of Node.js, just as Django is built on top of Python. Understanding what Node.js itself provides — and what it delegates to frameworks or userland libraries — is the foundation for using those tools well.

The significance of server-side JavaScript goes beyond developer convenience. Node.js was designed from the start around a specific insight: most web servers spend most of their time waiting — for a database to return a row, for a disk read to finish, for an upstream API to respond. Traditional server models (one thread per request) pay the full cost of that waiting in both memory and context-switching overhead. Node.js sidesteps this cost entirely by never waiting at all.

This 'never block' philosophy means a single Node.js process can hold thousands of concurrent in-flight connections while consuming far less memory than a thread-per-connection model. For I/O-heavy workloads — REST APIs, real-time feeds, proxy services — this translates directly to lower infrastructure costs and higher throughput.

The second major effect is ecosystem unification. npm (Node Package Manager) hosts over two million packages and has become the largest software registry ever created. Code written for Node.js can frequently be shared directly with browser-side bundles, enabling teams to maintain a single shared library for validation logic, data transformations, or domain models without duplication.

one language covers frontend, backend, scripting, and tooling. Developers can move between layers without context switching. Logic like form validation or date formatting can be written once and shared by both the client bundle and the server.
the event-driven model handles tens of thousands of simultaneous connections on a single thread. A chat server, notification feed, or API gateway that mostly waits on I/O is a natural fit.

JavaScript objects and JSON are the same data format. Parsing, transforming, and emitting API payloads involves no serialization friction — unlike languages where JSON must be deserialized into typed objects and re-serialized on the way out.

npm gives access to the largest package registry in existence. Database drivers, auth libraries, test runners, HTTP clients, and virtually every utility needed for a production service are available and actively maintained.
Node.js processes start in milliseconds, making them suitable for serverless functions, CLI tools, and short-lived tasks where JVM or CLR startup overhead is a problem.
Node.js is used in production at Netflix, LinkedIn, PayPal, Shopify, and many others. Long-term maintenance is backed by the OpenJS Foundation.

the JavaScript interpreter runs on one thread. CPU-intensive operations — image encoding, cryptographic key generation, machine-learning inference — block the event loop, making the entire process unresponsive to new requests until the work completes. Worker threads mitigate this, but they add architectural complexity.
if the dominant cost in a service is processing time rather than I/O wait time, Node.js offers no concurrency advantage. Go, Java, or C++ will outperform it on CPU-bound benchmarks.

JavaScript's lack of static types makes large codebases harder to refactor confidently. Most serious Node.js projects adopt TypeScript to add a type layer, which requires a compilation step and additional tooling.
older Node.js code and some npm packages still use callbacks rather than promises or async/await. Mixing paradigms in the same codebase is a frequent source of confusion.

unlike languages where uncaught exceptions crash the thread safely, an uncaught exception in Node.js can bring down the entire process. Explicit error handling at every async boundary is non-negotiable in production.
Node.js is built on two core components: the

. V8 compiles JavaScript to native machine code using just-in-time (JIT) compilation and manages garbage collection. libuv is a C library that provides the event loop, asynchronous I/O, and a thread pool for operations the operating system cannot do asynchronously (like some file-system calls and DNS lookups on certain platforms).
The event loop is the heart of Node.js. It runs continuously, iterating through a fixed sequence of phases:
executes I/O callbacks deferred from the previous iteration.
internal bookkeeping; not used by application code.
the most important phase. Retrieves new I/O events; executes I/O-related callbacks. If the queue is empty, the loop waits here for new events up to a calculated timeout.
events for abruptly closed handles (e.g. a socket closed mid-transfer).
Between each phase, Node.js drains two special queues before moving on: the
). The nextTick queue is drained first, before even microtasks.

When application code calls an asynchronous operation — reading a file, making an HTTP request, querying a database — Node.js does not pause execution. It registers a callback and returns immediately, allowing the event loop to process other events while the OS handles the underlying I/O. When the I/O completes, the OS signals libuv, which enqueues the callback. On the next poll phase, the event loop picks it up and executes it.

Operations that cannot be made truly asynchronous at the OS level (certain filesystem operations, DNS resolution on some platforms) are routed through libuv's thread pool, which defaults to four threads. These threads perform the blocking work in the background without touching the main JS thread, and signal completion through the event loop when finished.

The diagram below shows how a Node.js process connects clients to I/O resources through its internal components. Multiple clients send concurrent requests; the event loop on a single thread coordinates all responses by delegating I/O to libuv and firing callbacks when each operation completes.

attrs.alt: Node.js architecture diagram showing V8, Event Loop, libuv, Callback Queue, and I/O resources

content.text: Client requests arrive at the Node.js process and are immediately handed to the event loop — no new thread is created per request.
executes JavaScript on the single thread: route handlers, business logic, response serialization.
When JS code issues an async I/O call (database query, file read, HTTP fetch), the request is delegated to
which uses OS async primitives or its thread pool.
Completed I/O operations queue a callback in the
. The event loop picks these up when the call stack is clear.
, etc.) are the Node.js standard library — thin JavaScript wrappers over the C++ bindings that talk to libuv and V8.
most API work is I/O-bound — reading from databases, calling upstream services, returning JSON. Node.js handles high concurrency here with minimal resources.
chat servers, live collaboration tools, notification systems, and multiplayer game backends benefit from Node's event-driven WebSocket support.
API and event-loop model make it natural to pipe large files or media without loading them fully into memory.
fast startup and low idle memory make Node.js efficient for short-lived functions (AWS Lambda, Cloudflare Workers use V8/Node-compatible runtimes).
the entire JavaScript ecosystem's build tooling (webpack, Vite, ESLint, Prettier, TypeScript) runs on Node.js.
video transcoding, image processing at scale, ML model inference, or any loop that runs for hundreds of milliseconds will block the event loop. A separate process or worker thread is required.
if most of a service's time is spent in database CPU (complex joins, stored procedures), the I/O non-blocking model gives no advantage over a threaded server.
Python with NumPy/SciPy or Fortran/C extensions are better choices for numerical algorithms.
The first step is installing Node.js. The recommended path for most developers is the official installer at nodejs.org, or a version manager like
(Node Version Manager) which allows switching between Node.js versions per project.

# Install nvm (Linux/macOS)
curl -o- https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nvm-sh/nvm/v0.39.7/install.sh | bash

# Install and use the LTS release
nvm install 20
nvm use 20
node --version   # v20.x.x
npm --version    # 10.x.x
Once Node.js is installed, a server is a plain JavaScript file — no framework required for a minimal case. The built-in
module provides everything needed to listen for TCP connections and parse HTTP requests:
Async file read in a request handler
This example reads a JSON config file asynchronously and returns it as an API response. It demonstrates the
Node.js handles multiple in-flight async operations simultaneously. The three fetches below start at the same time; total elapsed time is roughly the slowest single fetch, not their sum:

A production API gateway serving 5,000 concurrent connections and 2,000 HTTP requests per second on a single Node.js process is not unusual for I/O-bound workloads. The following shows the core request handler pattern used in such a service — an endpoint that fetches a user profile and their recent orders in parallel from Postgres:
The two database queries run concurrently inside
so the response time is bounded by the slower query, not their sum. The connection pool ensures no new TCP connection is opened per request.
Using synchronous I/O inside request handlers
CPU work on the main thread
Race conditions in async state mutations
Modern Node.js (v14+) and all major drivers return Promises. Async/await produces readable, debuggable code and eliminates callback pyramid patterns.
Handle every error at every async boundary.
to every floating Promise. Add a global
handler as a last-resort safety net that logs and exits gracefully.
for connection strings, API keys, ports, and feature flags. The
file during development without touching production infrastructure.
Use a connection pool for every database.
Opening a new connection per request multiplies latency and overwhelms the database server. Configure the pool size so that workers × pool_size stays within the database's
Require modules at the top of the file.
is synchronous and cached after the first call. Calling it inside a request handler re-executes the synchronous lookup on every request and signals intent incorrectly.
to stop accepting new connections, finish in-flight requests, drain DB pools, and exit cleanly. This is essential for Kubernetes rolling deployments and zero-downtime restarts.
A single Node.js process uses one CPU core. The built-in
module forks one worker process per core, each with its own event loop. The OS distributes incoming connections across workers:
For isolated CPU-intensive tasks (parsing, hashing, compression), the
module runs JavaScript on a real OS thread. Workers can share memory via
and communicate with the main thread via message passing, without forking a full OS process.
watch heap usage over time. V8's garbage collector pauses briefly on major GC cycles. Keep each process heap under ~1 GB to avoid long pauses; use
to set an explicit limit and crash predictably rather than silently consuming all RAM.
. Overcommitting the pool causes connections to queue and response times to spike.
pipe large files or database cursors through Node's
API rather than buffering the full response in memory. This keeps memory usage flat regardless of payload size.
measure event loop delay in production using
. Sustained lag above ~50ms indicates a blocking operation that needs investigation.
the default package manager bundled with Node.js. Used for installing dependencies, running scripts, and publishing packages to the registry.
an alternative with deterministic lock files, workspace support for monorepos, and plug-and-play (PnP) mode that eliminates
uses a content-addressable store to share packages across projects, dramatically reducing disk usage in monorepo setups.
watches the file system and restarts the Node.js process automatically when a source file changes — the standard development experience for Node.js servers.
run TypeScript files directly without a separate compilation step.
the most widely used Node.js web framework. Minimal, un-opinionated, and extremely well-documented with a massive middleware ecosystem.
a performance-focused framework with built-in schema validation (JSON Schema) and a plugin system. Outperforms Express in raw throughput benchmarks.
an opinionated TypeScript framework with decorators, dependency injection, and a module system. Well-suited for large teams that want Rails-style conventions.
ultra-lightweight, runs on Node.js and edge runtimes (Cloudflare Workers, Deno, Bun) with the same API surface.

# Install PM2 — the most common Node.js process manager
npm install -g pm2

# Start the app with automatic restart on crash
pm2 start server.js --name api

# Scale to all CPU cores
pm2 scale api max

# Persist across reboots (generates systemd/init.d config)
pm2 startup
pm2 save

# Monitor CPU and memory in real-time
pm2 monit
Server-side JavaScript is one of several viable backend platforms. The right choice depends on workload characteristics, team skills, and ecosystem requirements:
I/O throughput, real-time, npm ecosystem, fast startup
Data science, ML integration, readable code
GIL limits true parallelism; slower throughput than Go/Node
Multi-core CPU performance, very low latency, tiny static binaries
Enterprise tooling, type safety, mature ecosystem
JVM startup overhead (mitigated by GraalVM native image), higher memory baseline
Mature CMS ecosystem, simple shared-nothing deployment
No native real-time support, no shared memory between requests
Rapid prototyping, rich conventions, excellent ORM
GIL limits parallelism; slower raw throughput than Go or Node

For teams already fluent in JavaScript, Node.js eliminates context switching and toolchain fragmentation. For services where CPU utilization dominates over I/O wait, Go or Java extract more throughput per core.

Never run a Node.js server as root. Create a dedicated system user, bind to an unprivileged port (above 1024), and place a reverse proxy (Nginx, Caddy, a cloud load balancer) in front that handles TLS termination and port 443 binding.
JavaScript's dynamic nature makes it easy to pass unexpected types. Validate every value arriving from HTTP bodies, query strings, headers, or environment variables before using it. Libraries like
(JSON Schema) provide structured validation with clear error messages.

with user-supplied strings is a remote-code-execution vulnerability. Similarly, deserializing untrusted data with parsers that execute code during parsing (certain YAML parsers, the deprecated node-serialize) is dangerous. Only parse known, safe formats.

# Scan installed packages for known vulnerabilities
npm audit

# Automatically upgrade fixable packages
npm audit fix

# For stricter CI enforcement (fails if any vulnerabilities found)
npm audit --audit-level=moderate
in CI on every pull request. Tools like Snyk, Dependabot, and GitHub's built-in security scanning can automate this and open pull requests for fixes automatically.
The clearest analogy for Node.js's event-driven model is a busy restaurant with one highly efficient waiter.

A traditional blocking server works like a restaurant where each waiter handles exactly one table at a time: takes the order, walks to the kitchen, stands there waiting for the food to be prepared, then brings it back before touching the next table. Ten simultaneous tables require ten waiters. The server's cost scales linearly with the number of concurrent requests.

Node.js works like a single waiter who never stands idle. When table 1 orders, the waiter writes it down and hands it to the kitchen. Immediately — without waiting for the food — the waiter goes to table 2, takes their order, then table 3, table 4. When the kitchen rings a bell (an I/O completion event), the waiter delivers the food to the correct table. One waiter, one thread, serves dozens of tables simultaneously because the only time the waiter pauses is when all tables have been served and no bells are ringing.

The constraint that follows from this analogy is important: if the waiter is asked to do something slow themselves — like peeling 500 shrimp at tableside — every other table waits. This is exactly why CPU-bound operations must be offloaded to worker threads or separate processes. They are tasks the waiter cannot hand to the kitchen; they have to do them personally, blocking service to everyone else while they do.
Node.js is a JavaScript runtime, not a framework.
It embeds V8 and libuv to give JavaScript access to the OS: sockets, files, child processes, and more.
The event loop runs on a single thread and never blocks.
Async I/O is delegated to libuv; when it completes, a callback is queued and executed when the call stack is next empty.
Non-blocking I/O is the defining advantage.
A single Node.js process can hold thousands of concurrent in-flight connections because it never ties a thread up waiting for disk, network, or database responses.
CPU-intensive work blocks the event loop.
Offload heavy computation to worker threads or a separate process. Node.js is the wrong choice when the bottleneck is CPU time, not I/O wait.
Handle every error at every async boundary.
Unhandled Promise rejections crash the process in Node.js 15+. Use
with async/await and a global fallback handler.
The npm ecosystem is the largest in software.
Leverage it, but audit dependencies regularly — supply-chain attacks grow with every transitive dependency added.
Scale horizontally with cluster or a process manager.
One Node.js process uses one CPU core. Spawn one worker per core and distribute traffic across them to utilize all available hardware.
Modern Node.js and all major libraries support Promises. Async/await makes sequential logic and error handling far more readable than nested callbacks.
